revision.comment: Grundinfos
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t D21:t N14-N33:N33:N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''{{unicode|''rj ḫnt.t''
lies: ''tjms n.j ḫnt.t''}}
''Der Rote des Schiffs'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' ist die Bezeichnung eines [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptischen]] [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekans]], der drei [[Baktiu|Dekan-Sterne]] umfasste und zum altägyptischen [[Sternbild]] [[Schiff (Sternbild)|Schiff]] gehörte, welches in vielen Särgen auf den [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en sowie beispielsweise auch im Grab des [[Senenmut]] abgebildet ist und etwa auf gleicher Höhe des altägyptischen Sternbildes [[Schaf (Sternbild)|Schaf]] lag. Die auffälligsten ''rötlichen'' [[Stern]]e sind hierbei [[Antares]] und [[Wei (Stern)|Wei]] im Sternbild [[Skorpion (Sternbild)|Skorpion]]. Der Name Antares stammt aus dem [[Griechische Sprache|Griechischen]] und bedeutet soviel wie ''„[[Ares]] entgegengesetzt“''. Der Gott [[Ares]] wurde von den [[Römisches Reich|Römer]]n [[Mars (Mythologie)|Mars]] genannt, und Antares hat sowohl eine ähnliche Farbe als auch ähnliche [[Scheinbare Helligkeit|Helligkeit]] wie [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]], weshalb beide leicht zu verwechseln sind, zumal sie sich stets in der Nähe der [[Ekliptik]] befinden. Der ägyptische Name „Der Rote des Schiffs“ bezieht sich auf die Gottheit [[Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Seth]], der auch als „Herr dieses Dekans“ betitelt wurde. In den Dekanlisten am Leib der [[Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Nut]] wurde Tjemes-ni-chentet in der Sethos-Schrift als 13. Dekan geführt; in der Anordnung unter [[Sesostris III.]] erschien Tjemes-ni-chentet als 12. Dekan, dessen [[heliakisch]]er Aufgang für den [[Schemu#Datierungen|26. Schemu IV]] angesetzt war. == Siehe auch == * [[Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, S. 62–67 und S. 385. [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = Tjemes-ni-chentet

lies: ''tjms n.j ḫnt.t''}}

Schemu IV]] angesetzt war. == Siehe auch == * [[Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. ''{{unicode|''rj ḫnt.t''
lies: ''tjms n.j ḫnt.t''}}
''Der Rote des Schiffs'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' ist die Bezeichnung eines [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptischen]] [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekans]], der drei [[Baktiu|Dekan-Sterne]] umfasste und zum altägyptischen [[Sternbild]] [[Schiff (Sternbild)|Schiff]] gehörte, welches in vielen Särgen auf den [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en sowie beispielsweise auch im Grab des [[Senenmut]] abgebildet ist und etwa auf gleicher Höhe des altägyptischen Sternbildes [[Schaf (Sternbild)|Schaf]] lag. Die auffälligsten ''rötlichen'' [[Stern]]e sind hierbei [[Antares]] und [[Wei (Stern)|Wei]] im Sternbild [[Skorpion (Sternbild)|Skorpion]]. Der Name Antares stammt aus dem [[Griechische Sprache|Griechischen]] und bedeutet soviel wie ''„[[Ares]] entgegengesetzt“''. Der Gott [[Ares]] wurde von den [[Römisches Reich|Römer]]n [[Mars (Mythologie)|Mars]] genannt, und Antares hat sowohl eine ähnliche Farbe als auch ähnliche [[Scheinbare Helligkeit|Helligkeit]] wie [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]], weshalb beide leicht zu verwechseln sind, zumal sie sich stets in der Nähe der [[Ekliptik]] befinden. Der ägyptische Name „Der Rote des Schiffs“ bezieht sich auf die Gottheit [[Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Seth]], der auch als „Herr dieses Dekans“ betitelt wurde. In den Dekanlisten der [[Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|Sethos-Schrift]] repäsentierte Tjemes-ni-chentet am Leib der [[Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Nut]] den zwölften Dekan. Der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang war für den [[Schemu#Datierungen|26. Schemu IV]] angesetzt und hatte als Datierungsgrundlage die verfügte Anordnung unter [[Sesostris III.]] ([[12. Dynastie]]) in dessen siebtem Regierungsjahr. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der Dekan-Sterne im Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, S. 62–67 und S. 385. [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

{{unicode|''rj ḫnt.t''
lies: ''tjms n.j ḫnt.t''}}
''Der Rote des Schiffs'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' ist die Bezeichnung eines [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptischen]] [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekans]], der drei [[Baktiu|Dekan-Sterne]] umfasste und zum altägyptischen [[Sternbild]] [[Schiff (Sternbild)|Schiff]] gehörte, welches in vielen Särgen auf den [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en sowie beispielsweise auch im Grab des [[Senenmut]] abgebildet ist und etwa auf gleicher Höhe des altägyptischen Sternbildes [[Schaf (Sternbild)|Schaf]] lag. Die auffälligsten ''rötlichen'' [[Stern]]e sind hierbei [[Antares]] und [[Epsilon Scorpii]] im Sternbild [[Skorpion (Sternbild)|Skorpion]]. Der Name Antares stammt aus dem [[Griechische Sprache|Griechischen]] und bedeutet so viel wie ''„[[Ares]] entgegengesetzt“''. Der Gott [[Ares]] wurde von den [[Römisches Reich|Römer]]n [[Mars (Mythologie)|Mars]] genannt, und Antares hat sowohl eine ähnliche Farbe als auch ähnliche [[Scheinbare Helligkeit|Helligkeit]] wie [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]], weshalb beide leicht zu verwechseln sind, zumal sie sich stets in der Nähe der [[Ekliptik]] befinden. Der ägyptische Name „Der Rote des Schiffs“ bezieht sich auf die Gottheit [[Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Seth]], der auch als „Herr dieses Dekans“ betitelt wurde. In den Dekanlisten der [[Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|Sethos-Schrift]] repräsentierte Tjemes-ni-chentet am Leib der [[Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Nut]] den zwölften Dekan. Der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang war für den [[Schemu#Datierungen|26. Schemu IV]] angesetzt und hatte als Datierungsgrundlage die verfügte Anordnung unter [[Sesostris III.]] ([[12. Dynastie]]) in dessen siebtem Regierungsjahr. == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, S. 62–67 und S. 385. [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

Der Gott [[Ares]] wurde von den [[Römisches Reich|Römer]]n [[Mars (Mythologie)|Mars]] genannt, und Antares hat sowohl eine ähnliche Farbe als auch ähnliche [[Scheinbare Helligkeit|Helligkeit]] wie [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]], weshalb beide leicht zu verwechseln sind, zumal sie sich stets in der Nähe der [[Ekliptik]] befinden. In den Dekanlisten der [[Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|Sethos-Schrift]] repräsentierte Tjemes-ni-chentet am Leib der [[Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Nut]] den zwölften Dekan. Der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang war für den [[Schemu#Datierungen|26. Schemu IV]] angesetzt und hatte als Datierungsgrundlage die verfügte Anordnung unter [[Sesostris III.]] ([[12. Dynastie]]) in dessen siebtem Regierungsjahr. == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. lies: ''Tjms-n.- ḫnt.t''
''Der Rote des Schiffs'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' ist die Bezeichnung eines [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptischen]] [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekans]], der drei [[Baktiu|Dekan-Sterne]] umfasste und zum altägyptischen [[Sternbild]] [[Schiff (Sternbild)|Schiff]] gehörte, welches in vielen Särgen auf den [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en sowie beispielsweise auch im Grab des [[Senenmut]] abgebildet ist und etwa auf gleicher Höhe des altägyptischen Sternbildes [[Schaf (Sternbild)|Schaf]] lag. Die auffälligsten ''rötlichen'' [[Stern]]e sind hierbei [[Antares|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)]] und [[Epsilon Scorpii|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)]] im Sternbild [[Skorpion (Sternbild)|Skorpion]]. Der Name Antares stammt aus dem [[Griechische Sprache|Griechischen]] und bedeutet so viel wie ''„[[Ares]] entgegengesetzt“''. Der Gott Ares wurde von den [[Römisches Reich|Römer]]n [[Mars (Mythologie)|Mars]] genannt, und Antares hat sowohl eine ähnliche Farbe als auch ähnliche [[Scheinbare Helligkeit|Helligkeit]] wie [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]], weshalb beide leicht zu verwechseln sind, zumal sie sich stets in der Nähe der [[Ekliptik]] befinden. Der ägyptische Name „Der Rote des Schiffs“ bezieht sich auf die Gottheit [[Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Seth]], der auch als „Herr dieses Dekans“ betitelt wurde. In den Dekanlisten der [[Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|Sethos-Schrift]] repräsentierte Tjemes-ni-chentet am Leib der [[Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Nut]] den zwölften Dekan. Der [[heliakisch]]e Aufgang war für den [[Schemu#Datierungen|26. Schemu IV]] angesetzt und hatte als Datierungsgrundlage die verfügte Anordnung unter [[Sesostris III.]] ([[12. Dynastie]]) in dessen siebtem Regierungsjahr. == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, S. 62–67 und S. 385. [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

Dynastie]]) in dessen siebtem Regierungsjahr. == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, S. 89. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|Diagonal star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr}}s and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern}}s in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]]

The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decade (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. ''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{illChristian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. ''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. ''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tjemes-ni-chentet-qq]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tjemes-ni-chentet-qq]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tjemes-ni-chentet-qq]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. ''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tjemes-ni-chentet}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tjemes-ni-chentet}}

''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=draft}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=draft}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=draft}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars|12=draft}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=draft}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)|12=draft}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache|12=draft}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich|12=draft}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars|12=draft}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars|12=draft}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik|12=draft}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=draft}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=draft}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=draft}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie|12=draft}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tjemes-ni-chentet}}

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Nut|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{citation needed|Tjemes-ni-chentet}}

The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich|12=draft}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars|12=draft}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars|12=draft}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik|12=draft}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=draft}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=draft}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|12=draft}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=draft}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|12=draft}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie|12=draft}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[Category:11]] }} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{citation needed|Tjemes-ni-chentet}}

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|qq=draft}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars|qq=draft}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)|qq=draft}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache|qq=draft}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich|qq=draft}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars|qq=draft}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars|qq=draft}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik|qq=draft}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie|qq=draft}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars|qq=draft}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)|qq=draft}}. {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|qq=draft}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars|qq=draft}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)|qq=draft}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache|qq=draft}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich|qq=draft}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars|qq=draft}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars|qq=draft}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik|qq=draft}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie|qq=draft}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egypt|qq=}} {{ill|Decan|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|1=Decan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan|qq=}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constellation|qq=}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senenmut|qq=}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|de|Stern|lt=stars|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Star|qq=}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|de|Skorpion (Sternbild)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scorpius|qq=}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Ancient Greek|de|Griechische Sprache|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Greek|qq=}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Roman Empire|de|Römisches Reich|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman Empire|qq=}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|de|Mars (Mythologie)|lt=Mars|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mars (mythology)|qq=}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)|lt=Mars|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mars (planet)|qq=}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|de|Ekliptik|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ecliptic|qq=}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|de|Heliakisch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heliacal rising|qq=}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|de|Sesostris III.|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senusret III|qq=}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|de|12. Dynastie|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|qq=}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] }} [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[en:draft:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn: failed]]

''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|de|Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. revision.text:
{{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] }} [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] }} [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''.
