revision.comment: Text replacement - "Wikidata entry [[d:" to "Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] }} [[sn:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decan Stars]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tjemes-ni-chentet]][[Category:qq]]

In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Matching pagename on wikidata]]

This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=}}. Schemu IV|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. ''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. ''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. revision.text:
[[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]][[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] }} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

revision.text:
[[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2803584]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox hieroglyphs |NAME = D21-M17-M17 W17:t*t N14-N33-N33-N33 |NAME-TRANSKRIPTION = Tjemes-ni-chentet
''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an [[:en:Ancient Egypt|Ancient Egypt]] [[:en:Decan|Decan]], which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]]. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" [[:en:Star|stars]] in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation [[:en:Scorpius|Scorpius]]. The name Antares comes from [[:en:Greek language|Ancient Greek]], meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the [[:en:Ancient Rome|Roman Empire]] as [[:en:Mars (mythology)|Mars]], and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to [[:en:Mars|Mars]]. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the [[:en:Ecliptic|Ecliptic]]. The [[:en:Heliacal rising|Heliacal rising]] was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under [[:en:Senusret III|Senusret III]] ([[:en:Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt]]) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * [[:en:Christian Leitz|Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. ''rj-ḫnt.t''
read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt}} {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|WD=Q5248738|lt=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|en|Senenmut|WD=Q328732|lt=Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|en|Star|WD=Q523|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|en|Scorpius|WD=Q8865|lt=Scorpius}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Greek language|en|Greek language|WD=Q9129|lt=Ancient Greek}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Ancient Rome|en|Ancient Rome|WD=Q1747689|lt=Roman Empire}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|en|Mars (mythology)|WD=Q112|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars|en|Mars|WD=Q111|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|en|Ecliptic|WD=Q79852|lt=Ecliptic}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q719639|lt=Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|WD=Q1080802|lt=Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt}} {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|WD=Q5248738|lt=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|en|Senenmut|WD=Q328732|lt=Senenmut}}. It was located approximately at the same height as the Ancient Egyptian constellation {{ill|Sheep (constellation)|de|Schaf (Sternbild)|lt=Sheep|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sheep (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}. The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|en|Star|WD=Q523|lt=stars}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|en|Scorpius|WD=Q8865|lt=Scorpius}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Greek language|en|Greek language|WD=Q9129|lt=Ancient Greek}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Ancient Rome|en|Ancient Rome|WD=Q1747689|lt=Roman Empire}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|en|Mars (mythology)|WD=Q112|lt=Mars}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars|en|Mars|WD=Q111|lt=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|en|Ecliptic|WD=Q79852|lt=Ecliptic}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q719639|lt=Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|WD=Q1080802|lt=Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

The most prominent "reddish" {{ill|Star|en|Star|WD=Q523|lt=stars|1=Star}} in this decan are {{ill|Antares|de|Antares (Alpha Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Antares|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} and {{ill|Epsilon Scorpii|de|Wei (Epsilon Scorpii)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epsilon Scorpii|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} in the constellation {{ill|Scorpius|en|Scorpius|WD=Q8865|lt=Scorpius|1=Scorpius}}. The name Antares comes from {{ill|Greek language|en|Greek language|WD=Q9129|lt=Ancient Greek|1=Greek language}}, meaning "opposite Ares." The god Ares was known to the {{ill|Ancient Rome|en|Ancient Rome|WD=Q1747689|lt=Roman Empire|1=Ancient Rome}} as {{ill|Mars (mythology)|en|Mars (mythology)|WD=Q112|lt=Mars|1=Mars (mythology)}}, and Antares has a similar color and apparent brightness to {{ill|Mars|en|Mars|WD=Q111|lt=Mars|1=Mars}}. This resemblance often leads to confusion, especially since both are located near the {{ill|Ecliptic|en|Ecliptic|WD=Q79852|lt=Ecliptic|1=Ecliptic}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795|1=Set (deity)}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." In the decan lists of the {{ill|Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|lt=Pharaonic period|de|Nutbuch#Pharaonische Zeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Nut#Pharaonic period|qq=|WD=Q2005559|1=Book of Nut}}, Tjemes-ni-chentet represented the 12th decan on the body of {{ill|Nut (Egyptian mythology)|de|Nut (Ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Nut (goddess)|lt=Nut|lt=Nut|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (Egyptian mythology)|13=User:Immanuelle/Nut (goddess)|qq=|WD=Q165496}}. The {{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|WD=Q368373|lt=Heliacal rising|1=Heliacal rising}} was set for the {{ill|Season of the Harvest|lt=Shemu#Dating|lt=26th Shemu IV|de|26. Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III|1=Senusret III}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q719639|lt=Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|1=Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|WD=Q1080802|lt=Christian Leitz|1=Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9, p. 89. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704|1=Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5, pp. 62–67 and 385. {{translated page|de|Tjemes-ni-chentet|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[ja:チェメス・ニ・チェンテト]] [[de:Tjemes-ni-chentet]] {{wikidata link|Q2803584}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Decans]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:pages]]

read as: ''Tjms-n.-ḫnt.t''
''The Red One of the Ship'' }} '''Tjemes-ni-chentet''' is the designation of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}} {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|WD=Q5248738|lt=Decan|1=Decan}}, which comprised three {{ill|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|de|Baktiu|1=Baktiu|2=de|3=Baktiu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baktiu|qq=|WD=Q804527}} (decan stars) and belonged to the Ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation|1=Constellation}} {{ill|Ship (constellation)|de|Schiff (Sternbild)|lt=Ship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ship (constellation)|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, depicted in many coffins on {{ill|star clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|star clock=star clock|lt=Diagonal star clocks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star clock|qq=|WD=Q142353|1=Star clock}} and, for example, in the tomb of {{ill|Senenmut|en|Senenmut|WD=Q328732|lt=Senenmut|1=Senenmut}}. The Egyptian name "The Red One of the Ship" refers to the deity {{ill|Set (deity)|de|Seth (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Set|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Set (deity)|qq=|WD=Q131795|1=Set (deity)}}, who was also titled "Lord of this Decan." Schemu IV|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Harvest|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}}, based on the arrangement ordered under {{ill|Senusret III|en|Senusret III|WD=Q19248|lt=Senusret III|1=Senusret III}} ({{ill|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|WD=Q719639|lt=Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt|1=Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt}}) in his seventh year of reign. == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|WD=Q1080802|lt=Christian Leitz|1=Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''.
